confidence_rationale: Authoritative TCS review of the fast-vs-slow meta-RL framework, by the team that produced both Wang 2018 and (Botvinick was supervisor for) the broader meta-RL-as-PFC research programme. The paper synthesises four years of post-2015 work on what makes deep RL more sample-efficient (episodic-memory augmentation, meta-learning, model-based RL); for MECH-318 the relevance is the explicit discussion of how recurrent hidden-state can serve as a fast-learning substrate that the slow weight-learning shapes. Confidence 0.78 reflects the secondary-review status (synthesis rather than primary anchor) balanced against authoritative source and direct on-topic content.
failure_signatures: An REE substrate that builds MECH-318 without coupling to episodic-memory retrieval (the fast-recall complement to slow weight-learning) will produce a less sample-efficient agent than Botvinick et al.'s framework predicts.

If MECH-318 is implemented as a pure-recurrent-meta-RL substrate without episodic-memory augmentation, the agent should show the deep-RL sample-inefficiency that Botvinick et al. anchor as the motivating problem.

If the broader fast-and-slow framework is the right reading for MECH-318, then the substrate should integrate with the existing REE episodic-memory substrates (MECH-269 anchor sets, MECH-292/293 ghost-goal bank) rather than displacing them.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Deep reinforcement learning, despite its impressive achievements, is famously slow to learn -- requiring far more experience than animals or humans need. Two complementary research directions address this. The episodic-memory direction (fast lookup) augments the agent with explicit memory retrieval, allowing rapid behaviour on recurring situations. The meta-learning direction (slow algorithmic shaping) trains the weights of a recurrent network across a task family so that the network's hidden-state dynamics implement an efficient learning algorithm. The two directions are complementary and the strongest deep-RL agents combine them.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-318 (rule-state abstraction substrate) is best positioned within a fast-and-slow architecture rather than as a pure meta-RL substrate. The 'slow' shaping is the dopaminergic / outer-loop training that installs the rule-state encoding in the recurrent dynamics (the Wang 2018 reading). The 'fast' retrieval is the episodic-memory mechanism that allows rapid response to recurring situations (in REE: the existing MECH-269 anchor sets, MECH-292/293 ghost-goal substrates). The architectural reading is that MECH-318 should COUPLE TO the existing episodic substrates rather than displace them, with the meta-RL recurrent-state and the episodic-memory retrieval playing complementary roles.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Botvinick et al. 2019 is a review of deep-RL research directions, not a substrate-prescriptive paper. The fast-and-slow framework is normative -- it says the best agents combine episodic memory with meta-RL -- but does not specify how the coupling should work at the substrate level. For REE the implication is that MECH-318 should be designed to compose cleanly with the existing episodic substrates rather than as a standalone module. Whether the composition is at the trigger layer (episodic retrieval signals when the meta-RL substrate should be active), at the readout layer (both substrates feed into action selection), or at the training layer (episodic substrates supply training data for the meta-RL substrate) is a substrate-design call that this review does not by itself resolve.
mapping.source_context: Trends in Cognitive Sciences 23(5):408-422 (2019). Botvinick + Ritter + Wang + Kurth-Nelson + Blundell + Hassabis -- all DeepMind. Synthesis review of post-2015 deep-RL advances written by the team most responsible for them. Authoritative cognitive-neuroscience-tradition treatment of meta-RL.

confidence_components.notes: TCS is high-quality; the DeepMind team is the dominant anchor for the research programme. Mapping fidelity good for the fast-and-slow architectural reading. Transfer risk moderate because the synthesis is at the framework level rather than the substrate level, leaving substrate-design choices to be made downstream.
